lists.title: Romanian A1 Grammar Path
lists.description: A1 grammar in communicative order: pronouns, three genders (including the neuter), the indefinite article, the suffixed definite article, a fi and a avea, the conjugation families, negation, existence, questions, and adjective agreement.

points.title: Subject pronouns (eu, tu, el…)
points.function: Say who you are talking about — when it needs saying
points.explanation: Eu (I), tu (you, informal), el/ea (he/she), noi (we), voi (you all), ei/ele (they m/f), plus polite dumneavoastră. The verb ending names the person, so Romanian usually drops the pronoun — vorbesc românește — and uses it for emphasis.
points.culture_note: Politeness has tiers: dumneata (semi-formal) and dumneavoastră (formal, takes plural verbs). Start formal with strangers.

drills.translation: I am from Bucharest.
You are my friend.
She is my sister.
We are students.
They are from Spain.
You (all) are very kind.
He is my brother.
They are my friends (all women).
I speak a little Romanian.
Do you live here?
He works at a bank.
She sings very beautifully.
We live in Cluj.
Do you (all) speak English?
They play football.
They (f.) arrive tomorrow.

points.title: Gender and number — including the neuter
points.function: Name people and things, one or many
points.explanation:
Romanian kept Latin's THREE genders.

The neuter is a shape-shifter: masculine in the singular, feminine in the plural (un scaun → două scaune).

Plurals vary: -i (băiat → băieți), -e (casă → case), -uri (tren → trenuri) — learn each noun with its plural.
points.culture_note: The neuter covers most objects — a huge slice of everyday vocabulary follows the un…/două… pattern.

drills.translation: I have two books.
I have three sisters.
I have two cats in the house.
I have two new chairs.
I have two brothers.
I am buying two apples.
Two children are singing in the park.

points.title: Indefinite articles (un / o)
points.function: Mention something for the first time
points.explanation: Un before masculine AND neuter singulars (un câine, un scaun), o before feminine (o casă). The gender shows again in 'two': doi câini but două scaune, două case.

drills.translation: I have a dog.
It is a big house.
I have an idea.
It is a good book.
I have a new chair.
drills.hint: neuter takes the masculine article in the singular

drills.translation: It is a beautiful day.

points.title: The definite article is a suffix (-ul / -a)
points.function: Point to a specific thing — the Romanian way
points.explanation:
Romanian is the only Romance language that GLUES 'the' onto the end of the noun: scaun (chair) → scaunul (THE chair), casă → casa, carte → cartea.

Masculine/neuter take -ul or -le, feminines swap -ă for -a. One word, article included.
points.culture_note: This suffixed article is a Balkan areal feature Romanian shares with Bulgarian and Albanian — not something it inherited from Latin.

drills.translation: The house is big.
The chair is new.
The book is interesting.
The coffee is hot.
The teacher works here.
The door is open.

points.title: A fi — present (sunt, ești, este…)
points.function: Say who or what someone is
points.explanation:
A fi (to be): sunt, ești, este (e in speech), suntem, sunteți, sunt.

Sunt doubles for 'I am' and 'they are' — the pronoun or context disambiguates. Professions take no article: ea este profesoară.

drills.translation: I am a student.
You are very tall.
She is a teacher.
We are friends.
Are you (all) Romanian?
They are at home.
I am from Iași.
Are you a doctor?
My father is an engineer.
We are ready.
Are you (all) tired?
My parents are from Brașov.

points.title: A avea — present (am, ai, are…)
points.function: Say what you have — and how old you are
points.explanation:
A avea (to have): am, ai, are, avem, aveți, au.

Age is HAD: am douăzeci de ani (I am twenty). So is being right: ai dreptate (you're right).

drills.translation: I am twenty years old.
Do you have a cat?
She has a question.
We have time.
You (all) are right.
They have a house in the mountains.
I have two sisters.
Do you have a pen?
My brother has a dog.
We have a small house.
Do you (all) have children?
They have many friends.

points.title: Present tense of -a verbs
points.function: Talk about what people do
points.explanation:
First-conjugation verbs end in -a.

Plain type: a intra → intru, intri, intră, intrăm, intrați, intră.

Many take -ez: a lucra → lucrez, lucrezi, lucrează.

Both are everywhere — learn each verb's type with the verb.

drills.translation: I work in an office.
Do you listen to music every day?
She sings beautifully.
They play football.
You (all) are entering the house.
I am buying bread.
We work together.
You work too fast!
He listens to old music.
We work in the evening.
Do you (all) come in here often?
They play tennis.

points.title: -ea, -e and -i verbs (a vedea, a merge, a dormi)
points.function: See, go, sleep, read — the other families
points.explanation:
A merge (to go): merg, mergi, merge, mergem, mergeți, merg.

A dormi: dorm, dormi, doarme.

A vedea: văd, vezi, vede.

Some -i verbs take -esc: a citi → citesc, citești, citește — the Romanian cousin of Italian's -isc-.

drills.translation: I go to school.
We are writing a letter.
They sleep a lot.
She is reading a book.
Do you hear the music?
Do you (all) see the sea from here?
I am writing an email.
Do you drink coffee in the morning?
She sees well from here.
We are going to the market.
Do you (all) sleep late?
They read the newspaper.

points.title: Negation with nu
points.function: Say what is not so
points.explanation: Nu goes directly before the verb: nu vorbesc engleză, nu am timp. It's also the word for 'no' — one negator for everything.

drills.translation: I don't speak English.
He doesn't work on Sundays.
We are not tired.
She doesn't eat meat.
Aren't you eating?
You (all) don't dance?

points.title: Este / sunt — there is, there are
points.function: Say what exists or is available
points.explanation: Romanian just uses 'to be': Pe masă este o carte (On the table there is a book), În stradă sunt două cafenele. Singular este, plural sunt — no special phrase to memorize.

drills.translation: There is a book on the table.
There are two cafés in the street.
There is a cat in the garden.
There is no milk.
Are there many problems?
There is a beautiful park in the city.

points.title: Question words (unde, ce, când…)
points.function: Ask about places, people, time, and reasons
points.explanation: Unde (where), ce (what), când (when), cine (who), cum (how), de ce (why), cât/câți (how much/many). Question word first, verb next: Unde locuiești?

drills.translation: Where do you live?
What is your name?
When is your birthday?
What is this?
How many brothers do you have?
Who is your teacher?

points.title: Adjective agreement
points.function: Describe people and things correctly
points.explanation:
Most adjectives have four forms: frumos, frumoasă, frumoși, frumoase.

They agree with the noun and usually FOLLOW it: o casă albă. Neuter nouns take masculine adjectives in the singular, feminine in the plural — the shape-shifting again.

drills.translation: The house is white.
The books are new.
My sister is very kind.
The film is interesting.
The flowers are beautiful.
The boy is tall.
The shoes are black.
The windows are open.

points.title: The past — perfectul compus
points.function: Talk about completed past actions
points.explanation:
The everyday past is a compound: a short form of 'a avea' (to have) + the invariable past participle.

The auxiliary is special here — am, ai, a, am, ați, au — and never changes for the participle: eu am scris, tu ai scris, el a scris, noi am mers, voi ați văzut, ei au plecat.

The participle stays the same for everyone.

drills.translation: I wrote a letter yesterday.
This morning I drank my coffee quickly.
Have you read this book?
When did you get home last night?
He missed the morning train.
Maria sang a beautiful song.
This summer we were at the seaside.
Last night we ate together at the restaurant.

drills.sentence: La ce oră {{answer}} sosit la petrecere?
drills.translation: What time did you (pl.) arrive at the party?
Have you (pl.) already finished the project?

drills.sentence: Copiii {{answer}} jucat în curte toată ziua.
drills.translation: The children played in the yard all day.
The students studied for the exam.

points.title: The genitive-dative case
points.function: Show possession and the indirect object
points.explanation:
Romanian merges the genitive ('of') and dative ('to') into one case. For people's names it uses lui before masculine names (cartea lui Ion 'Ion's book') and an -i ending on feminine ones (Mariei).

Nouns show it through the article: băiatul → băiatului, fata → fetei, and the plural ending -lor (copiilor 'of/to the children').

drills.translation: This is Ion's book.
I gave the gift to a child.
The colour of the sea is blue.
I told the truth to my sister.
The children's toys are on the floor.
I sent a letter to my parents.

points.title: Plural of nouns
points.function: Make nouns plural
points.explanation: Plural endings depend on gender: masculine usually adds -i (băiat → băieți, often with sound changes); feminine adds -e or -i (casă → case, floare → flori); neuter — nouns that are masculine in the singular but feminine in the plural — take -uri or -e (scaun → scaune, tren → trenuri).

drills.translation: I have two good friends at school.
There are many flowers in the garden.
There are three books on the table.
We need more chairs.
The children got new toys.
I bought two train tickets.

points.title: The imperfect (imperfectul)
points.function: Describe ongoing or habitual past actions
points.explanation:
The imperfect sets a scene or describes a repeated/ongoing past action — English 'was …-ing' or 'used to'.

It has one set of endings for all conjugations: -am, -ai, -a, -am, -ați, -au (scriam 'I was writing', mergeam 'I used to go').

Contrast it with the perfect compound, which reports one completed act.

drills.translation: Every summer we used to go to our grandparents'.
When I was little, I used to read a lot.
What were you doing when I called you?
Grandfather used to tell stories every evening.

drills.sentence: Copiii se {{answer}} în parc toată ziua.
drills.translation: The children were playing in the park all day.
In the old days we lived in a small apartment.

points.title: The future (voi scrie / o să scriu)
points.function: Talk about what will happen
points.explanation:
Romanian has two common futures. The formal one is voi/vei/va/vom/veți/vor + the infinitive: voi scrie 'I will write'.

The everyday spoken one is o să + subjunctive: o să scriu. Both mean the same; o să is more colloquial and invariable (o să scriu, o să scrii, o să scrie…).

drills.translation: Tomorrow I will go to work early.
Tonight we will watch a film together.
Will you come with us on the trip?
I'll buy the gift tomorrow.

drills.sentence: Anul viitor {{answer}} învăța română în fiecare zi.
drills.translation: Next year I will study Romanian every day.
My friends will be at the party.

points.title: Direct object pronouns (mă, te, îl, o…)
points.function: Replace the object with a short pronoun
points.explanation:
A known direct object becomes a weak pronoun before the verb: mă (me), te (you), îl (him/it m.), o (her/it f.), ne (us), vă (you pl.), îi/le (them).

Îl văd 'I see him', o cunosc 'I know her'. In the perfect, most attach as l-am, te-am, iar 'o' goes after: am văzut-o.

drills.sentence: Îl cunosc pe Ion; {{answer}} văd în fiecare zi.
drills.translation: I know Ion; I see him every day.
Where is Maria? I can't find her.
What a good book! I'm reading it now.
If you come, I'll wait for you at the stop.
We're here; can you hear us?

drills.sentence: Copiii sunt obosiți; {{answer}} duc la culcare.
drills.translation: The children are tired; I'm taking them to bed.

points.title: Comparative & superlative (mai… decât, cel mai)
points.function: Compare things
points.explanation:
The comparative is mai + adjective + decât 'than': mai mare decât 'bigger than'. The superlative adds the article cel/cea/cei/cele before mai: cel mai mare 'the biggest'.

The article agrees in gender and number with the noun.

drills.translation: Bucharest is bigger than Iași.

drills.sentence: Aceasta este cea {{answer}} bună cafenea din oraș.
drills.translation: This is the best café in town.

drills.sentence: Româna este {{answer}} grea decât spaniola pentru mine.
drills.translation: Romanian is harder than Spanish for me.

drills.sentence: El este {{answer}} mai inteligent elev din clasă.
drills.translation: He is the smartest student in the class.
In summer the days are longer.
Health is more important than money.

points.title: Prepositions (în, la, cu, pe, pentru)
points.function: Say where, with, for — and mark a personal object
points.explanation:
Common prepositions: în 'in', la 'at/to', cu 'with', de 'of/from', pentru 'for', fără 'without'.

After most, a singular noun DROPS its definite article (în oraș 'in town'). The preposition pe also marks a definite personal direct object: îl văd pe Ion 'I see Ion'.

drills.translation: I live in a small town.
I go to school every day.
I'm coming to the cinema with my friends.
I bought a gift for my mother.
I can't live without coffee.
I see Maria at the market.

points.title: Possessives (meu, mea, tău…)
points.function: Say whose something is
points.explanation:
Possessives follow the noun and agree with it in gender and number: al meu / a mea / ai mei / ale mele 'my', tău/ta 'your', său/sa 'his/her', nostru/noastră 'our', vostru/voastră 'your', lor 'their'.

With a definite noun the linking article often merges: cartea mea 'my book', prietenii mei 'my friends'.

drills.translation: My car is new.
Where is your phone?
His/her sister lives in Cluj.
His dog is very friendly.
Our neighbours are very kind.
Their house is near the sea.

points.title: Reflexive verbs (a se spăla)
points.function: Say the subject acts on itself
points.explanation:
Reflexive verbs carry a pronoun that matches the subject: mă, te, se, ne, vă, se — placed before the verb.

Mă spăl 'I wash (myself)', te trezești 'you wake up', se odihnește 'he/she rests'. Many everyday verbs are reflexive in Romanian (a se gândi 'to think', a se simți 'to feel').

drills.sentence: În fiecare dimineață {{answer}} trezesc la șapte.
drills.translation: Every morning I wake up at seven.

drills.sentence: La ce oră {{answer}} culci de obicei?
drills.translation: What time do you usually go to bed?

drills.sentence: Ea {{answer}} simte mult mai bine azi.
drills.translation: She feels much better today.
On Sundays we rest at home.
Did you (pl.) have fun at the party?
The children are playing in the park.

points.title: The subjunctive (conjunctivul cu să)
points.function: Say what you want, must, or can do
points.explanation:
Romanian rarely uses the infinitive after another verb — instead it uses să + subjunctive, agreeing with the subject: vreau să plec 'I want to leave', trebuie să înveți 'you must study'.

In the 3rd person the subjunctive has its own form: el merge → să meargă, ea face → să facă, este → să fie.

Negate the second verb with să nu.

drills.translation: I want to leave earlier today.
You must study for the exam.

drills.sentence: El trebuie să {{answer}} la doctor mâine.
drills.translation: He must go to the doctor tomorrow.
It's important that we be careful.
I want to know Romanian well.

drills.sentence: Ea vrea să {{answer}} un tort pentru petrecere.
drills.translation: She wants to make a cake for the party.

points.title: The imperative (scrie! scrieți! nu scrie!)
points.function: Give direct commands and prohibitions
points.explanation:
The singular command often equals the 'you' present form (scrii → scrie!, mergi → mergi!), while the plural uses the -ți form (scrieți!, mergeți!).

Negative singular commands are special: nu + infinitive (nu scrie!, nu pleca!); negative plural uses nu + the -ți form (nu plecați!).

drills.translation: Open the window, please!
Speak more quietly, the child is sleeping.
Children, open your books to page ten!
Be careful when you cross the street!

drills.sentence: Nu {{answer}} griji, totul va fi bine.
drills.translation: Don't worry, everything will be fine. (nu-ți face griji)
Don't leave yet, we still have time!

points.title: Dative pronouns (îmi, îți, îi…)
points.function: Say 'to me / to you' with a short pronoun
points.explanation:
The indirect object ('to whom') has its own weak pronouns: îmi (to me), îți (to you), îi (to him/her), ne (to us), vă (to you pl.), le (to them).

Îmi place cafeaua 'coffee pleases me = I like coffee', îți dau cartea 'I give you the book'. In the perfect they contract: mi-a dat, ți-am spus.

drills.translation: I really like music. (music pleases me)
I'll give you the book tomorrow, I promise.
Maria is sad; she misses her family.
The teacher explains the lesson to us clearly.

drills.sentence: Dacă aveți întrebări, {{answer}} răspund cu plăcere.
drills.translation: If you (pl.) have questions, I'll gladly answer you.

drills.sentence: Copiilor {{answer}} place să se joace afară.
drills.translation: The children like to play outside.

points.title: Relative pronouns (care, ce, cine)
points.function: Join sentences with 'who / which / that'
points.explanation: The main relative is care, invariable for gender/number but taking pe when it's a personal object (omul pe care îl cunosc 'the man whom I know') and forms like căruia/căreia in the genitive-dative. ce means 'what/which' (tot ce știu 'all that I know'); cine means 'who' standing alone (cine știe 'who knows').

drills.translation: The man who came is a doctor.

drills.sentence: Filmul pe {{answer}} l-am văzut a fost minunat.
drills.translation: The film that I saw was wonderful.
Everything you say is true.
Whoever works hard succeeds.

drills.sentence: Copiii {{answer}} se jucau afară au intrat.
drills.translation: The children who were playing outside came in.

drills.sentence: Aceasta este casa în {{answer}} am crescut.
drills.translation: This is the house in which I grew up.

points.title: The conditional (aș scrie)
points.function: Say what you would do
points.explanation:
The conditional is a special auxiliary — aș, ai, ar, am, ați, ar — plus the infinitive: aș merge 'I would go', ar veni 'he would come'. It expresses wishes and hypotheticals and is the polite way to ask: aș vrea un cafea 'I would like a coffee', ai putea să mă ajuți?

'could you help me?'.

drills.translation: I would like a coffee, please.
Could you help me a little?
He would gladly come to the party.

drills.sentence: Noi {{answer}} merge la munte cu plăcere.
drills.translation: We would gladly go to the mountains.
They would like to live by the sea.
In your place, I would accept the offer.

points.title: Prepositions with genitive-dative (datorită, în fața)
points.function: Use prepositions that govern the genitive or dative
points.explanation: A set of prepositions takes the genitive-dative rather than the plain noun: datorită 'thanks to' + dative, contra / împotriva 'against' + genitive, and compound phrases with a possessive twist — în fața 'in front of', în jurul 'around', din cauza 'because of' — all followed by a genitive: în fața casei 'in front of the house'.

drills.translation: I succeeded thanks to your help.
The car is parked in front of the house.
Everyone voted against the proposal.
Because of the rain, we stayed home.
The children run around the table.
He won thanks to his work.

points.title: Reported speech (că, dacă)
points.function: Report what someone said or asked
points.explanation:
Report a statement with că + a full clause: a spus că vine 'he said (that) he's coming'. Report a yes/no question with dacă 'whether': m-a întrebat dacă vin 'he asked me whether I'm coming'.

A command becomes să + subjunctive: mi-a zis să aștept 'he told me to wait'. Romanian keeps the original tense.

drills.translation: He told me that he'll be a bit late.
He asked me whether I want a coffee.
The teacher said to read the chapter.
I think that he is right.
I asked her to call me tonight.

drills.sentence: Nu știu {{answer}} magazinul e deschis azi.
drills.translation: I don't know whether the shop is open today.

points.title: The passive voice (este scris / se vinde)
points.function: Say an action is done, without naming the doer
points.explanation:
Romanian has two passives. The analytic one is a fi + past participle, which agrees with the subject: cartea este scrisă 'the book is written', ușile sunt închise 'the doors are closed'.

The reflexive passive uses se and is very common for general statements: aici se vinde pâine 'bread is sold here', se spune că… 'it is said that…'.

drills.translation: The book is written in Romanian.
The doors are closed at nine o'clock.
Fresh bread is sold here.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} spune că iarna va fi grea.
drills.translation: It is said that the winter will be harsh.

drills.sentence: Casa a {{answer}} construită acum o sută de ani.
drills.translation: The house was built a hundred years ago.
The problems are solved easily with patience.

points.title: Conditional sentences (dacă…)
points.function: Say 'if… then…' — real and unreal
points.explanation:
Real conditions use dacă + present/future: dacă înveți, vei reuși 'if you study, you'll succeed'.

Unreal (hypothetical) conditions pair dacă + imperfect with the conditional: dacă aș avea timp, aș veni 'if I had time, I would come'.

Past-unreal uses the past conditional in both: dacă aș fi știut, ți-aș fi spus.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} înveți mult, vei reuși la examen.
drills.translation: If you study a lot, you'll pass the exam.

drills.sentence: Dacă aș avea timp, aș {{answer}} la petrecere.
drills.translation: If I had time, I would come to the party.

drills.sentence: Dacă {{answer}} bani, ar cumpăra o casă.
drills.translation: If he had money, he would buy a house.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} plouă mâine, nu mergem în excursie.
drills.translation: If it rains tomorrow, we won't go on the trip.

drills.sentence: Dacă aș fi știut, {{answer}} fi spus imediat.
drills.translation: If I had known, I would have told you at once.

drills.sentence: Ai reuși {{answer}} ai încerca mai mult.
drills.translation: You would succeed if you tried harder.

points.title: The gerund (gerunziul: scriind)
points.function: Say 'while doing' something
points.explanation:
The gerund ends in -ând or -ind (scriind 'writing', mergând 'going', citind 'reading') and is invariable.

It describes an action happening alongside the main one — English '…-ing': a plecat cântând 'he left singing', citind ziarul, am adormit 'reading the paper, I fell asleep'.

drills.translation: He entered the room smiling.
Running for the bus, I fell.
He spent the evening reading a novel.
By listening carefully, you'll learn fast.
He left saying goodbye.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} pe stradă, am întâlnit un prieten.
drills.translation: Walking down the street, I met a friend.

points.title: The supine (de scris, ușor de făcut)
points.function: Express 'to be done' and purpose
points.explanation:
The supine looks like the past participle preceded by de (or a preposition): am ceva de scris 'I have something to write', e ușor de făcut 'it's easy to do', mașină de spălat 'washing machine'.

It names an action abstractly, like an English '…-ing' noun or a 'to-'infinitive, and never agrees.

drills.translation: I have many things to do today.
This lesson is easy to understand.
I still have one chapter to read.
We have a dishwasher. (machine for washing dishes)
There's nothing to see here.
The food is ready to serve.

points.title: Subjunctive after impersonals (trebuie să, e posibil să)
points.function: Express necessity, possibility, and emotion
points.explanation:
Impersonal expressions are followed by să + subjunctive: trebuie să 'one must', e posibil să 'it's possible that', e bine să 'it's good to', îmi pare rău că / să 'I'm sorry that'.

The subjunctive also follows conjunctions of purpose (ca să 'in order to') and doubt.

drills.translation: We must leave now, it's late.
It's possible that it will rain tonight.

drills.sentence: Învăț mult ca {{answer}} reușesc la examen.
drills.translation: I study a lot in order to pass the exam.
It's good to drink a lot of water.
I'm afraid I might be late.

drills.sentence: Ar fi bine să {{answer}} mai devreme mâine.
drills.translation: It would be good if we came earlier tomorrow.

points.title: Comparative structures (tot atât de, din ce în ce)
points.function: Express equality and gradual change
points.explanation: Beyond mai…decât, Romanian marks equality with (tot) atât de … ca / la fel de … ca 'as … as': e la fel de înalt ca mine 'he's as tall as me'. Gradual change uses din ce în ce mai 'more and more' (din ce în ce mai greu 'harder and harder') and cu cât … cu atât 'the more … the more'.

drills.sentence: El este la {{answer}} de înalt ca mine.
drills.translation: He is as tall as me.

drills.sentence: Vremea devine din ce în ce mai {{answer}}.
drills.translation: The weather is getting colder and colder.
drills.hint: cold (din ce în ce mai = more and more)

drills.sentence: Cu cât citești mai mult, {{answer}} atât înveți mai bine.
drills.translation: The more you read, the better you learn.

drills.sentence: Nu e {{answer}} atât de greu pe cât pare.
drills.translation: It's not as hard as it seems.

drills.sentence: Orașul este {{answer}} fel de frumos ca în poze.
drills.translation: The city is as beautiful as in the photos.

drills.sentence: Zilele devin din ce în ce mai {{answer}}.
drills.translation: The days are getting longer and longer.

points.title: The pluperfect (mai-mult-ca-perfectul)
points.function: Set one past action before another
points.explanation:
The pluperfect ('had done') is a single synthetic form — no auxiliary — with endings -sem, -seși, -se, -serăm, -serăți, -seră on the participle stem: scrisesem 'I had written', plecase 'he had left', veniserăm 'we had come'.

It marks an action completed before another past point.

drills.translation: When you arrived, I had already eaten.

drills.sentence: Trenul {{answer}} deja când am ajuns la gară.
drills.translation: The train had already left when I got to the station.

drills.sentence: Nu am recunoscut casa; se {{answer}} mult.
drills.translation: I didn't recognize the house; it had changed a lot.

drills.sentence: Până atunci {{answer}} deja despre acest oraș.
drills.translation: By then I had already read about this city.

drills.sentence: Ei {{answer}} plecat înainte să înceapă ploaia.
drills.translation: They had left before the rain started.

drills.sentence: Îmi amintii că îl {{answer}} undeva înainte.
drills.translation: I remembered that I had seen him somewhere before.

points.title: The past conditional (aș fi scris)
points.function: Say what would have happened
points.explanation:
The past conditional adds fi + the participle to the conditional auxiliary: aș fi scris 'I would have written', ar fi venit 'he would have come'.

It states an unreal past outcome, usually in a dacă-aș-fi clause: dacă aș fi știut, aș fi venit 'if I had known, I would have come'.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} fi venit imediat, dar nu am știut nimic.
drills.translation: I would have come at once, but I knew nothing.

drills.sentence: El {{answer}} fi reușit cu puțin efort.
drills.translation: He would have succeeded with a little effort.

drills.sentence: Noi {{answer}} fi mers, dar era prea târziu.
drills.translation: We would have gone, but it was too late.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} fi vrut să te ajut, dar nu am putut.
drills.translation: I would have liked to help you, but I couldn't.

drills.sentence: Ce {{answer}} fi făcut tu în locul meu?
drills.translation: What would you have done in my place?

drills.sentence: Ei {{answer}} fi câștigat cu puțin noroc.
drills.translation: They would have won with a little luck.

points.title: Subordinating connectors (deși, întrucât, astfel încât)
points.function: Build complex sentences: concession, cause, result
points.explanation:
Formal Romanian links clauses precisely: deși / cu toate că 'although', întrucât / deoarece 'because/since', astfel încât / așa încât 'so that (result)', pe măsură ce 'as', în timp ce 'while'.

Each introduces a subordinate clause with its own verb.

drills.translation: Although it was late, we continued the discussion.
I stayed home because it was raining hard.

drills.sentence: A vorbit tare, {{answer}} încât toți l-au auzit.
drills.translation: He spoke loudly, so that everyone heard him.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} timp ce eu găteam, el citea.
drills.translation: While I was cooking, he was reading.

drills.sentence: Pe {{answer}} ce înaintam, drumul devenea mai greu.
drills.translation: As we advanced, the road became harder.

drills.sentence: Nu a venit, {{answer}} toate că îl invitasem.
drills.translation: He didn't come, although I had invited him.

points.title: Clitic doubling (pe Ion îl văd, îi dau Mariei)
points.function: Double a full object with a matching pronoun
points.explanation:
Romanian systematically 'doubles' a definite object with a clitic. A personal direct object takes pe and a matching accusative pronoun: pe Ion îl văd 'I see Ion', o cunosc pe Maria 'I know Maria'.

An indirect object is doubled with a dative pronoun: îi dau Mariei cartea 'I give Maria the book'. The doubling is obligatory, not emphatic.

drills.sentence: Pe Ion {{answer}} văd în fiecare zi.
drills.translation: I see Ion every day.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} cunosc pe Maria de mult timp.
drills.translation: I've known Maria for a long time.
I'll give Maria the book tomorrow.
I told the children the story.
It was you (pl.) I'd been waiting for.
I often help my neighbours.

points.title: Register & the literary layer (perfectul simplu, formal style)
points.function: Shift between formal/literary and everyday Romanian
points.explanation:
Written and literary Romanian differs from speech. The perfectul simplu (a synthetic past — scrise 'he wrote', merse 'he went') survives in literature and in Oltenian speech, where the compound past dominates elsewhere.

Formal registers prefer connectors like așadar 'therefore', întrucât 'inasmuch as', precum și 'as well as', and fixed phrases (în ceea ce privește 'as regards').

drills.sentence: Se făcu liniște, iar el {{answer}} încet spre ușă.
drills.translation: Silence fell, and he walked slowly to the door. (literary past)
The old man told a story and fell silent. (literary past)

drills.sentence: Nu a studiat destul; {{answer}}, a picat examenul.
drills.translation: He didn't study enough; therefore, he failed the exam.

drills.sentence: În ceea ce {{answer}} bugetul, vom discuta mâine.
drills.translation: As regards the budget, we'll discuss it tomorrow.
The teachers came, as well as the students.
Although the weather was bad, the show went on. (literary)
